content.assessment: Splitting grade structures partially addresses cost incidence by concentrating the highest-cost grades (upper primary, requiring subject specialists and more infrastructure) into larger units while retaining lower-cost, high-value proximity grades locally. This aligns with evidence that younger children bear the largest harms from commuting, making the mitigation of transport-related opportunity costs more targeted. But the proposal remains exposed to national class-size minimums that could still make retained lower-grade schools fiscally non-viable, and reversal risk (as in Poland 2017) generates severe adjustment costs — displaced teachers, unplaced cohorts — that constitute a distinct fiscal category from ordinary consolidation costs.
opportunities: A staged/graded approach could be evaluated for whether it captures more of the claimed savings from upper-grade consolidation while avoiding the largest share of documented transport harms and offset costs
strengths: Targets fiscal and welfare costs more precisely by grade level rather than treating all pupils uniformly

Structural retention of lower grades may reduce the transportation cost offsets documented as reducing net consolidation savings (F-live-education_finance-03), since younger pupils are the largest transport-cost/harm group
threats: Reversal risk, as demonstrated in Poland, creates concentrated fiscal costs (teacher displacement, cohort disruption) that are large and lumpy relative to gradual policy stability
Multigrade teaching quality requirements imply additional teacher-training investment cost not currently budgeted, per the mechanism description
weaknesses: Retained lower-grade schools remain fiscally exposed to national class-size minimums regardless of structural reorganization (per mechanism description), a risk not resolved by this lens's evidence base

Contested international evidence on whether consolidation produces net savings (F-live-education_finance-02) applies equally to the upper-grade consolidation component, so expected savings are uncertain
